entries.title: Intruder overview
entries.body:
# Intruder overview

**Intruder** is the Burp tool for automatically sending large numbers of HTTP requests, where different values (payloads) are substituted into pre-selected locations. Essentially you take one "base" request, mark exactly what should be iterated over, define a set of values, and Burp runs through all the combinations on its own, collecting the responses in a table for analysis.

If **Repeater** is "send one request by hand and look at the response", then **Intruder** is "send a thousand variations of the same request and compare the responses against each other".

## What it is used for

* **Fuzzing** - injecting special characters and SQLi/XSS/path traversal payloads into a parameter to find a vulnerability based on the application's reaction.
* **Iterating over identifiers (IDOR)** - sequentially substituting `id=1`, `id=2`, `id=3`... to find access to other people's objects.
* **Authentication brute-force** - guessing login/password/PIN/token.
* **Finding hidden parameters, directories, headers** - iterating over a wordlist of names.
* **Data harvesting** - extracting tokens, names, e-mails and other data that changes from request to request out of responses.

## What an attack consists of

Any **Intruder** attack is described by four things:

1. **Base request (Positions)** - an HTTP request template with `§` markers placed in it, between which Burp will substitute payloads.
2. **Attack type** - the algorithm by which values are distributed across positions: **Sniper**, **Battering ram**, **Pitchfork**, **Cluster bomb**.
3. **Payloads** - the sets of values to substitute, their type (list, numbers, dates, etc.), processing rules and encoding.
4. **Settings** - what to look for in responses (grep-match, grep-extract), flags for redirects; performance parameters (number of threads, delays) are configured via the **Resource pool**.

## How to launch it in a minute

```
1. In Proxy > HTTP history find the request you need.
2. Right-click > Send to Intruder (or Ctrl+I).
3. Go to Intruder. Burp will open a new tab with this request.
4. Positions tab: place § markers around what you want to iterate over.
5. Payloads tab: choose a type and add values.
6. Click Start attack - a window with the results table will open.
```

## Important note about licensing

In **Burp Suite Community Edition**, **Intruder** works with artificial throttling - a delay is inserted between requests, and large attacks go very slowly. This is done deliberately so the tool cannot be used as a full-fledged brute-force in the free version. Full speed is available only in **Burp Suite Professional / Enterprise**. For ARS3NAL training labs this is fine: small payload sets run even in Community, just more slowly.

entries.title: Payload positions (§)
entries.body:
# Payload positions (§)

A payload position is a place in the base request where, during the attack, **Intruder** will substitute values. Each position is marked with a pair of **§** markers (the paragraph symbol), and Burp replaces the text inside them with the next payload. Everything outside the markers stays unchanged in every request.

Positions are configured on the **Positions** tab inside the attack tab.

## How to place markers

1. Open the attack tab in **Intruder**, go to **Positions**.
2. Use the mouse to select the fragment of the request you want to iterate over (for example, a parameter value).
3. Click **Add §** - the selected fragment gets wrapped in two markers and is highlighted.

Example: it was

```http
POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: target
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=admin&password=secret
```

it became (iterating over the password):

```http
POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: target
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=admin&password=§secret§
```

The text `secret` inside `§…§` is just a "placeholder" for the base request; during the attack it will be replaced with values from the payload set.

## Position control buttons

* **Add §** - wrap the selected fragment in markers (add a position).
* **Clear §** - remove all markers from the request (start with a clean slate).
* **Auto §** - automatic placement: Burp itself finds typical changeable locations (URL parameter values, body, cookies) and wraps them. Handy as a starting point, but afterwards it is better to remove the unnecessary positions by hand.

## Placement subtleties

* **Wrap only the value, not the parameter name.** A marker on `password=§secret§`, not on `§password=secret§` - otherwise you will be iterating over the key itself, which is rarely what you need.
* **An empty position is normal.** You can put two markers in a row `§§` with no text between them: during the attack a payload will simply be inserted there "from scratch".
* **Literal § character.** If the paragraph symbol actually appears in the request as data, escape it so Burp does not mistake it for a position marker (insert `\§`).
* **The number of positions matters for the attack type.** With one position all attack types work the same way and simply; the differences between **Sniper**, **Pitchfork** and **Cluster bomb** appear exactly when there are several positions.
* **Encoding.** Take into account which context the value will land in (URL, JSON, form body). The payload encoding itself is configured separately (see the section on payload processing), and the base request is shown "as is".

## In practice

When fuzzing a single parameter, one position and the **Sniper** type are enough. When guessing a login+password pair, place two positions and choose **Cluster bomb** (try all combinations) or **Pitchfork** (move in step, in pairs). Always check that no extra markers are left over: every extra position multiplies (or by several times increases) the number of requests.

entries.title: Attack types - Sniper, Battering ram, Pitchfork, Cluster bomb
entries.body:
# Attack types: Sniper, Battering ram, Pitchfork, Cluster bomb

The **Attack type** determines how **Intruder** lays out values across the `§` positions. It is selected from a drop-down list on the **Positions** tab. As long as there is one position, there is almost no difference; everything becomes important when there are several positions.

Below, a "set" means a separate payload list (payload set). Let us agree: set 1 = `[a, b]`, set 2 = `[1, 2]`.

## Sniper (default)

A single shared payload set. Burp iterates over the positions **in turn**: it substitutes a value into the first position (leaving the others with their original value from the base request), goes through the whole set, then moves on to the second position, and so on.

* Number of requests = (number of payloads) × (number of positions).
* When to use: fuzzing a single parameter; or checking several parameters with the **same** wordlist, but **independently** of each other.

Example: positions A and B, set `[a, b]`. Requests: `A=a`, `A=b`, `B=a`, `B=b` (the "silent" position gets its base value). That makes 2×2 = 4 requests, but these are NOT combinations - each time only one position changes.

## Battering ram

A single shared set. The next value is substituted **into all positions at once**.

* Number of requests = number of payloads.
* When to use: when the same value must appear in several places of the request at once. The classic case is a session token/identifier that is duplicated both in the cookie and in the body; or the same username that is needed in several fields.

Example: positions A and B, set `[a, b]`. Requests: `A=a, B=a`, then `A=b, B=b`. That makes 2 requests.

## Pitchfork

Its own separate set for **each** position. Burp goes through the sets **in sync, in step**: the 1st element of set 1 is placed in position 1 at the same time as the 1st element of set 2 in position 2, then the second elements, and so on. The iteration stops at the shortest set.

* Number of requests = length of the shortest set.
* When to use: when values are linked in pairs and order matters. For example, you have a ready list of `user:csrf_token` pairs or `id:matching_hash`, and you need to send exactly "the first with the first, the second with the second".

Example: position A with set `[a, b]`, position B with set `[1, 2]`. Requests: `A=a, B=1`, then `A=b, B=2`. That makes 2 requests (there are NO crosswise combinations).

## Cluster bomb

Its own set for each position, but Burp iterates over **all possible combinations** of values between the sets (the Cartesian product).

* Number of requests = product of the lengths of all sets.
* When to use: classic credential guessing, when the correspondence of login and password is **unknown** and you need to check each password with each login. In general - any "try all combinations of several parameters" situation.

Example: position A with set `[a, b]`, position B with set `[1, 2]`. Requests: `A=a,B=1` · `A=a,B=2` · `A=b,B=1` · `A=b,B=2`. That makes 2×2 = 4 requests.

## Selection cheat sheet

```
One parameter, fuzzing                -> Sniper
One value into all positions at once   -> Battering ram
Several parameters, linked in pairs    -> Pitchfork
Several parameters, all combinations   -> Cluster bomb
```

The main beginner trap is **Cluster bomb** with large wordlists: 1000 logins × 1000 passwords = 1,000,000 requests. Before launching, always estimate the final number (Burp shows the request count counter) and shrink the sets if necessary.

entries.title: Payload types
entries.body:
# Payload types

The **Payload type** determines where the substituted values come from and how they are generated. It is selected from the **Payload type** drop-down list in the **Payloads** side panel. For each position (in **Pitchfork** / **Cluster bomb**) you can define its own set and its own type.

Below are the main types and when they are useful.

## Simple list

A simple list of strings that you enter manually, load from a file, or pull in from Burp's built-in lists. The most common type.

```
admin
root
password123
' OR '1'='1
../../../../etc/passwd
```

Where it is needed: fuzzing, wordlist guessing, any ready-made lists.

## Runtime file

Reads payloads line by line from a file **during the attack**, without loading the whole file into memory. Indispensable for very large wordlists (millions of lines), where a **Simple list** would "eat up" all the RAM.

## Numbers

Generates numbers in a given range with a given step and format (decimal/hexadecimal, with leading zeros, fractional). Ideal for iterating over IDs.

```
From: 1   To: 500   Step: 1   -> 1,2,3,...,500
```

Where it is needed: ID enumeration (IDOR), iterating over PINs/codes.

## Dates

Generates dates in a range with a given step (day/month/year) and output format. Useful for iterating over date parameters, date-based tokens, day-by-day exports.

## Brute forcer

Full enumeration: generates all permutations of characters from a given alphabet of a given length. Grows combinatorially - suitable only for short values (a short PIN, a 4-character captcha).

```
Alphabet: abc   Length 2 -> aa,ab,ac,ba,bb,...,cc
```

## Null payloads

Generates empty values ("nothing"). The payload itself does not change - the only thing that changes is that the same request is sent N times. Used for race condition attacks, for checking token randomness, and for load checks.

## Character substitution

Takes a list of strings and applies character substitutions to each one according to a given table (for example, `a->@`, `o->0`, `i->1`). Handy for generating "leet" password variants from a base wordlist.

## Case modification

Applies case variations to each string of the list (all lowercase, all uppercase, first letter capitalized, etc.). Useful when guessing passwords and when bypassing case-sensitive filters.

## Recursive grep

Extracts text from the response to the **previous** request and substitutes it into the **current** one. Requires a pre-configured grep-extract rule. The classic scenario is a "rolling" CSRF token: each response hands back a new token that must be used in the next request. Because of the "request after request" dependency it works only in a single thread.

## Username generator

From a list of names or e-mail addresses, generates likely logins using common schemes (`ivan.petrov`, `ipetrov`, `petrov_i`, `i.petrov`, and so on). Speeds up building a login wordlist for guessing.

## Custom iterator

A constructor that builds payloads as combinations of up to several "slot positions" by a template, with a separate value list and separators for each slot. Lets you generate values of a complex structure (for example, `<prefix>-<number>-<suffix>`) without an external file.

## Character blocks / Character frobber / Bit flipper

A group of "low-level" types:

* **Character blocks** - repeats a character/string in blocks of a given length (useful for finding overflows and length limits).
* **Character frobber** - changes each character of the base value in turn, shifting its ASCII code. Helps to see which exact character affects processing (for example, signature validity).
* **Bit flipper** - inverts the bits of the value one by one. Used in attacks on cryptography (for example, on CBC) and when analyzing token robustness.

## ECB block shuffler

Reorders ciphertext blocks in data encrypted in ECB mode, to influence the decrypted plaintext. A narrowly specialized type for ECB crypto attacks.

## Illegal Unicode

Replaces a specified character with its illegal (overlong) Unicode representations. Used to bypass filters and WAFs that compare by exact byte.

## Copy other payload

Copies the value from another position of the same attack into the current one. Needed when one value must appear in several places, but the other positions are iterated over independently.

## Collaborator payloads

Generates unique **Burp Collaborator** payloads for detecting out-of-band interactions (for example, blind SSRF/SQLi). Each payload is a unique subdomain, and requests to it are caught by Collaborator.

## Extension-generated

Delegates payload generation to an installed Burp extension. Lets you use custom logic from a BApp or your own plugin.

> The availability of some types and features (for example, Collaborator) depends on the Burp edition. In Community some capabilities are limited.

entries.title: Payload processing and encoding
entries.body:
# Payload processing and encoding

**Payload processing** is the set of rules applied to each generated value **before** it goes into the request. They let you modify payloads on the fly without editing the wordlist itself. Rules are configured in the **Payload processing** block on the **Payloads** tab.

## How it works

* Rules run **in order, top to bottom** - it is a pipeline: the output of one rule becomes the input of the next.
* The list of rules can be enabled/disabled with a checkbox and reordered, changing the processing order.
* Next to it is a separate encoding setting - final URL-encoding of selected characters (see below).

## Main rules

### Add prefix / Add suffix
Adds fixed text before/after the payload. Handy for building a value up to the required format.

```
payload:  admin
Add prefix:  user_
Add suffix:  @corp.local
result:  user_admin@corp.local
```

### Match/replace
Replaces the parts of the payload that matched a regular expression with a given string. For example, to remove spaces or insert a separator.

```
Match (regex):  \s+
Replace with:   _
"john doe"  ->  "john_doe"
```

### Substring
Takes a substring out of the payload by position and length (cuts out the needed piece).

### Encode / Decode
Encodes or decodes the value in a chosen scheme: URL, HTML, Base64, ASCII hex, etc. Often used to pack a payload to fit the parameter format.

```
payload:  ' OR '1'='1
Encode -> Base64:  JyBPUiAnMSc9JzE=
```

### Hash
Hashes the payload with a chosen algorithm (for example, MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256). Needed when the application expects the value not in cleartext but as a hash (for example, guessing passwords that are compared by hash).

```
payload:  password
Hash -> MD5:  5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99
```

### Add raw payload
Adds the original (unprocessed) version of the payload to the processed value. Useful when you need to send a payload both "raw" and transformed at the same time (for example, a password plus its hash).

### Skip if matches regex
Checks the current (already processed) value against a regex and, if it matches, **skips** this payload, moving on to the next one. This lets you filter out obviously unnecessary values from a large wordlist.

```
Skip if matches:  ^.{0,5}$
Effect: skip all values shorter than 6 characters
(for example, under a minimum password length policy)
```

### Invoke Burp extension
Passes the payload to an installed extension for arbitrary processing with its own logic.

## Payload encoding (URL-encode)

Separately from the processing rules there is a **URL-encode these characters** setting. It automatically URL-encodes the listed "dangerous" characters (`& = + ; #` and so on) in the final payload, so they do not break the structure of the HTTP request. It is usually enabled by default - disabling it is something to do consciously, when you deliberately want to send a character in raw form.

## In practice

A typical chain for guessing passwords by hash: a password wordlist (**Simple list**) -> the **Hash (MD5)** rule -> sending. For careful fuzzing with special characters: a payload wordlist -> if necessary, **Encode (URL)** or the enabled URL-encoding of characters, so the payload reaches the application untouched. Always check the order of rules: for example, the value must be encoded AFTER adding the prefix/suffix, otherwise the wrong thing gets encoded.

entries.title: Analyzing results
entries.body:
# Analyzing results

After **Start attack**, Burp opens the attack window with a **results table**: one row per request. The analyst's task is to quickly find the "anomalous" responses in this table - the ones that differ from the bulk of the rest, because those usually indicate success (a valid login, an existing ID, a payload that worked).

## Table columns

Each row by default shows:

* **Request** - the sequential number of the request.
* **Payload** - which value was substituted (for several positions - several Payload columns).
* **Status code** - the HTTP response code.
* **Length** - the length of the response in bytes.
* **Error / Timeout** - flags for a connection error or timeout.

Clicking any row opens the full **Request/Response** for that request at the bottom.

## Sorting and filtering

* **Sorting** - clicking a column header sorts the table. This is the main technique: sort by **Length** or **Status** and the outlying values gather at the top/bottom of the list.
* **Right-click on a header** - add/remove columns.
* **Filter** - the filter panel lets you show only the responses you need (for example, hide all `404`, keep `200` and `302`), filter by length range, etc.

## What to look at

* **Status code.** When guessing logins/directories, success is often = a different code: `200` amid a sea of `403`, or `302` (a redirect after a successful login) amid `200`.
* **Length.** The most reliable indicator. A "wrong password" page and a "welcome" page almost always differ in size. Sort by length and the outlier is visible at once.
* **Response time.** A sharply larger response time may indicate a time-based SQL injection, or that for an existing user the password is actually checked (and for a non-existent one - it is not).

## Grep - Match

At the configuration stage (**Settings**) you can define a list of marker strings. For each response Burp will add a column with a checkbox: whether this string is contained in the response. For example, search for `Welcome` or `Invalid` - and the column instantly shows which requests "passed".

```
Grep - Match:
  Welcome
  Invalid credentials
  error
```

## Grep - Extract

Lets you **pull out** a specific fragment from each response into a separate column: a token, a username, a balance, an error message. You specify the boundaries (between which markers the needed text lies) or a regex. Ideal for data harvesting: for example, run through a list of IDs and for each one pull the owner's name out of the response.

## Grep - Payloads

Marks responses in which the sent payload itself appears (its reflection). A direct helper in finding reflected XSS and other input-reflection vulnerabilities.

## Export

Results can be saved: choose the columns and separator you need and export to a file. It is especially useful to save a single column (for example, the extracted grep-extract values) - it can then be fed as an input wordlist to the next attack or to another tool.

## In practice

The analysis algorithm is almost always the same: (1) sort by **Length**, (2) if it is not obvious - sort by **Status**, (3) look at the outliers in the **Response** tab, (4) confirm the finding via **Repeater**. Pre-configured **Grep - Match** on the words "success/error" saves a ton of time on large attacks.

entries.title: Intruder use cases
entries.body:
# Intruder use cases

Below are typical practical tasks and how to solve them in **Intruder**. For each scenario the attack type, the payload type, and what to look at in the results are given.

## 1. Fuzzing a parameter

Goal: understand how the application reacts to special characters and malicious strings - this is the path to SQLi, XSS, path traversal, command injection.

```
Request:   GET /product?id=§1§
Attack:    Sniper
Payload:   Simple list (fuzzing wordlist)
           '
           "
           <script>alert(1)</script>
           ../../../../etc/passwd
           ;id
           ${{7*7}}
```

What to look at: spikes in **Length**, `500` codes, reflection of the payload in the response (**Grep - Payloads**), time delays. Re-check suspicious strings in **Repeater**.

## 2. Iterating over identifiers - IDOR / enumeration

Goal: check whether other people's objects are accessible by a predictable ID.

```
Request:   GET /api/account/§1§
Attack:    Sniper
Payload:   Numbers (from 1 to 1000, step 1)
Grep-Extract: pull the owner's name/e-mail out of the response
```

What to look at: where there is `200` instead of `403/404` - there you have gained access to someone else's record. The **Grep - Extract** column will immediately show whose data it is. This is the classic way to confirm **Broken Access Control / IDOR**.

## 3. Password brute-force (login fixed)

Goal: guess the password for a known user.

```
Request:   POST /login  (body: username=admin&password=§x§)
Attack:    Sniper
Payload:   Simple list (password wordlist), Hash if necessary
Grep-Match: Welcome / Invalid
```

What to look at: one response with a different **Length** or a `302` code amid many `200 Invalid` - that is the correct password.

## 4. Credential brute-force (login and password iterated)

Goal: guess a login+password pair when the correspondence is unknown.

```
Request:   POST /login  (username=§u§&password=§p§)
Attack:    Cluster bomb
Payload 1: Simple list - logins
Payload 2: Simple list - passwords
```

Important: **Cluster bomb** iterates over ALL combinations, the number of requests = logins × passwords - keep an eye on the volume. If you have ready `user:pass` pairs (for example, from a leak), use **Pitchfork** to go in pairs and not make extra requests.

What to look at: an outlier by **Length**/**Status**; confirm the login in **Repeater**.

## 5. Guessing hidden parameters

Goal: find undocumented GET/POST parameters that change the application's behavior.

```
Request:   GET /api/data?§param§=1
Attack:    Sniper
Payload:   Simple list (wordlist of parameter names: debug, admin, test,
           is_admin, role, redirect, file, ...)
```

What to look at: requests where the response differs from the base one in length/code/content - such a parameter was "recognized" by the application. Investigate the found parameter further separately (for example, for LFI/SSRF/privilege escalation).

## 6. Iterating over directories and endpoints

```
Request:   GET /§FUZZ§
Attack:    Sniper
Payload:   Runtime file (a large wordlist of paths)
Filter:    hide 404, keep 200/301/302/403
```

What to look at: everything that is not `404`. `403` is also interesting - the resource exists but is closed off.

## 7. Data harvesting

Goal: bulk-extract data from responses.

```
Request:   GET /user/§1§/profile
Attack:    Sniper
Payload:   Numbers (1..N)
Grep-Extract: email, name, role
Export:    save the Grep-Extract column to a file
```

What to look at: the **Grep - Extract** columns; the result can be exported and used as an input wordlist for the next attack.

## General recommendations

* Before launching, estimate the number of requests (especially for **Cluster bomb** and **Brute forcer**).
* First do a small "test" run on 5-10 values - make sure the positions and encoding are configured correctly, and only then launch the full wordlist.
* Confirm any finding from the table with a manual send to **Repeater**.
* Remember the throttling in Community Edition: heavy brute-force tasks are realistically run only in Professional.
